title: Non-Cooperative Games (Part II) â€” Congestion Games, Potential Games, and Price of Anarchy

questions.source_ref: Section 1: Motivation: Large Games and Concise Representations
questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why the notes introduce congestion games as a structured class of large games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An $n$-player game with $k$ actions per player generally needs $k^n$ numbers just to encode utilities
Congestion games are highlighted because they have concise descriptions despite potentially many players
The notes claim large games are easy to reason about once each player has the same number of actions
The motivation is to study games with more structure so rational analysis remains tractable
The notes argue that every large game can be reduced to a congestion game without loss

explanations.text: The notes explicitly use $k^n$ as the representation burden for an $n$-player, $k$-action game.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Motivation: Large Games and Concise Representations
explanations.notes_quote: To represent an $n$-player game where each player has $k$ actions, we need $k^n$ numbers just to encode the utility functions.

explanations.text: Congestion games are introduced as a prime example of large games with concise structure.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Motivation: Large Games and Concise Representations
explanations.notes_quote: Congestion games are a prime example.

explanations.text: The notes do not say equal action counts make large games easy; they say naive representation becomes unmanageable and motivates more structured models.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Motivation: Large Games and Concise Representations

explanations.text: The point of the section is that additional structure makes large games easier to reason about.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Motivation: Large Games and Concise Representations
explanations.notes_quote: we study games with substantially more structure despite being large â€” games with a concise description that makes them easy to reason about

explanations.text: The notes present congestion games as one important structured class, not as a universal reduction target for all large games.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Motivation: Large Games and Concise Representations

questions.text: Which components belong to the formal definition of a congestion game in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A set of players $N$
A set of facilities/resources $R$
For each player $i$, an action set $A_i$ whose actions are subsets of $R$
For each facility $j$, a delay function $d_j : \\{0, \\ldots, n\\} 	o \\mathbb{R}_{\\geq 0}$
A transfer rule that redistributes total congestion cost equally among all players

explanations.text: The player set is one of the four defining ingredients listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: A set of $n$ players $N$

explanations.text: Facilities/resources form the shared resource set of the congestion game.
explanations.notes_quote: A set of $m$ facilities (or resources) $R$

explanations.text: Each player's actions are subsets of the resource set, not arbitrary labels.
explanations.notes_quote: for each player $i$, a set of actions $A_i$ where each action $a_i \\in A_i$ represents a subset of the facilities: $a_i \\subseteq R$

explanations.text: Each resource has a congestion-dependent cost function over the number of users.
explanations.notes_quote: For each facility $j \\in R$, a cost (or delay) function $d_j : \\{0, \\ldots, n\\} 	o \\mathbb{R}_{\\geq 0}$

explanations.text: No equal redistribution rule appears in the definition; player cost is determined by the resources in their chosen action and the congestion on those resources.

questions.text: Which statements about player costs in congestion games are correct according to the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $n_j(a)$ counts how many players use facility $j$ under action profile $a$
Player $i$'s cost is $c_i(a) = \\sum_{j \\in a_i} d_j(n_j(a))$
A player's cost depends on the identities of the other users on each resource
The key dependence is on congestion levels, not who the other users are
A player pays for every resource in $R$, even if they do not choose it

explanations.text: The notes define $n_j(a)$ exactly as the number of players using facility $j$.
explanations.notes_quote: define $n_j(a) = |\\{i : j \\in a_i\\}|$ to be the number of players using facility $j$

explanations.text: The player's total cost is the sum of delays on the facilities in their chosen action.
explanations.notes_quote: The cost of agent $i$ is: $$c_i(a) = \\sum_{j \\in a_i} d_j(n_j(a))$$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say identities do not matter; only the number of users matters.
explanations.notes_quote: Each player's cost depends only on the congestion (number of users) on each resource they use, not on the identities of the other users.

explanations.text: This is the key structural simplification of congestion games.
explanations.notes_quote: depends only on the congestion (number of users) on each resource they use, not on the identities of the other users

explanations.text: The sum is only over resources in $a_i$, so unused resources do not contribute to player $i$'s cost.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe a network congestion game as presented in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It is also called a routing game
The resource set corresponds to the graph's edges
Each player's action set is the set of paths from their source to their target
Each player's action set is the set of all spanning trees of the graph
Players may have distinct source-target pairs $(s_i, t_i)$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify network congestion games with routing games.
explanations.notes_quote: In a network congestion game (aka routing game)

explanations.text: Edges are the resources whose congestion determines costs.
explanations.notes_quote: The resource set $R$ corresponds to the set of edges $E$

explanations.text: A player's feasible actions are exactly the paths between their designated endpoints.
explanations.notes_quote: $A_i$ is the set of paths from $s_i$ to $t_i$

explanations.text: The notes define actions as source-target paths, not spanning trees.
The formal definition allows each player to have their own source-target pair.
explanations.notes_quote: for each player $i \\in N$, there is a dedicated source-target pair $(s_i, t_i)$

questions.source_ref: Section 2.4: Worked Example: Three-Player Routing Game
questions.text: In the three-player routing example, which statements are true? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In State A, the social cost is 8
In State B, all three players incur cost 3
Player 1 prefers State A because 4 is less than 3
State B is the Nash equilibrium even though it has higher social cost than State A
State A is the social optimum

explanations.text: The notes compute State A's social cost as $4 + 2 + 2 = 8$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.4: Worked Example: Three-Player Routing Game
explanations.notes_quote: Social cost: $4 + 2 + 2 = 8$

explanations.text: When all three use the shared edge in State B, each pays 3.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.4: Worked Example: Three-Player Routing Game
explanations.notes_quote: All three players on $s_2 t_2$: cost 3 each

explanations.text: Player 1 prefers State B because their cost falls from 4 to 3, not the other way around.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.4: Worked Example: Three-Player Routing Game
explanations.notes_quote: Player 1 prefers State B (cost 3 < 4)

explanations.text: The example is designed to show equilibrium inefficiency: the equilibrium has higher social cost.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.4: Worked Example: Three-Player Routing Game
explanations.notes_quote: State B is the Nash equilibrium despite having higher social cost. State A is the social optimum.

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify State A as the socially optimal state.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.4: Worked Example: Three-Player Routing Game
explanations.notes_quote: State A is the social optimum

questions.text: Which of the following are listed application areas of congestion games in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Traffic routing and transportation networks
Telecommunication and communication networks
Cloud and distributed computing
Multi-agent systems and robotics
Electricity grids and smart energy systems
Only zero-sum board games with perfect information

explanations.text: Traffic routing is the first application area listed.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Traffic Routing and Transportation Networks
explanations.notes_quote: Drivers/vehicles choose routes, each aiming to minimise travel time.

explanations.text: Communication networks are explicitly named as a congestion-game application.
explanations.notes_quote: Data packets select routes or channels; performance (latency, bandwidth) decreases with congestion.

explanations.text: The notes include cloud and distributed computing as a direct application domain.
explanations.notes_quote: Tasks/jobs compete for computational resources (CPUs, memory, bandwidth).

explanations.text: Robotics and MAS are included because agents share infrastructure and bottlenecks.
explanations.notes_quote: Multiple robots/agents sharing physical infrastructure ... use congestion games to coordinate movement and avoid bottlenecks.

explanations.text: Smart energy systems are listed because costs rise with collective demand.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.5: Electricity Grids and Smart Energy Systems
explanations.notes_quote: Consumers/smart devices choose when to use electricity; costs rise with collective demand.

explanations.text: The notes list many real-world resource-sharing domains, not only zero-sum perfect-information games.

sections.title: 2. Braess's Paradox and Best Response Dynamics

questions.text: Which statements correctly capture Braess's Paradox as described in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Adding a new road can increase equilibrium travel time
Closing roads can sometimes improve traffic flow
The paradox says every new road always improves equilibrium outcomes
The notes mention Seoul's Cheonggyecheon highway closure as a real-world example
Braess's Paradox only applies when all edge costs are constant

explanations.text: This is the core statement of the paradox.
explanations.notes_quote: Adding a new road (resource) to a network can increase the total travel time at equilibrium.

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state the reverse effect can also happen.
explanations.notes_quote: Closing roads can sometimes speed up traffic.

explanations.text: Braess's Paradox is precisely the warning that extra capacity can worsen equilibrium, not always improve it.
The Seoul example is used to illustrate the paradox in practice.
explanations.notes_quote: Seoul, South Korea closed a major highway along the Cheonggyecheon Stream. Counter-intuitively, traffic flow actually improved

explanations.text: The paradox is illustrated with both congestion-dependent and constant edges; it is not restricted to constant-cost networks.

questions.text: In the Braess's Paradox worked example with 200 travelers, which statements are true? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Without the shortcut, the Nash equilibrium splits travelers 100 and 100 across the two routes
Without the shortcut, each traveler spends 30 minutes
With the free shortcut, all travelers taking $A 	o B 	o C 	o D$ yields 40 minutes each
With the shortcut, a unilateral deviation strictly lowers travel time below 40 minutes
The shortcut increases equilibrium travel time from 30 to 40 minutes

explanations.text: The no-shortcut equilibrium is an even split across the two routes.
explanations.notes_quote: Without shortcut (B-C road): Nash equilibrium: 100 travelers on each route

explanations.text: The notes compute $100/10 + 20 = 30$ minutes for each traveler.
explanations.notes_quote: Each traveler's time: $100/10 + 20 = 30$ min

explanations.text: With the free shortcut, the equilibrium routes everyone through the middle and each pays 40 minutes.
explanations.notes_quote: Nash equilibrium: ALL travelers take $A 	o B 	o C 	o D$ ... Each traveler's time: $200/10 + 200/10 = 40$ min

explanations.text: The notes say alternative routes also give 40 minutes, so there is no profitable unilateral deviation.
explanations.notes_quote: This is a NE because any alternative ... also gives 40 min

explanations.text: This is the punchline of the example: the added road worsens equilibrium travel time.
explanations.notes_quote: Adding the free road increased everyone's travel time from 30 to 40 minutes

questions.text: Which statements about best response dynamics are correct according to the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It repeatedly lets a player switch to a cost-minimizing action against the current profile
If it halts, the returned profile is a pure Nash equilibrium
The notes claim it always halts in every game
Matching Pennies is used as an example where best response dynamics can cycle
For congestion games, the notes say best response dynamics always halts

explanations.text: The algorithm updates a player to an action in their current best-response set.
explanations.notes_quote: Set $a_i = \\arg\\min_{a'\\in A_i} c_i(a, a_{-i})$

explanations.text: Halting means no player can improve, which is exactly the PSNE condition.
explanations.notes_quote: If best response dynamics halts, it returns a pure Nash equilibrium

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the answer is no in general, then single out congestion games as a positive case.
explanations.notes_quote: Does it always halt? In general, no

explanations.text: Matching Pennies is the counterexample used for cycling and lack of PSNE.
explanations.notes_quote: Matching Pennies has a cycle of profitable deviations and no PSNE

explanations.text: The notes contrast the general negative answer with the positive result for congestion games.
explanations.notes_quote: But for congestion games, yes.

questions.text: Which of the following are consequences of Rosenthal's theorem in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every sequence of improvement steps in a congestion game is finite
Congestion games have the Finite Improvement Property (FIP)
Every congestion game has at least one pure Nash equilibrium
FIP is weaker than mere existence of a PSNE
The theorem implies decentralized improvement can reach equilibrium from any starting profile

explanations.text: This is the theorem statement itself.
explanations.notes_quote: For every congestion game, every sequence of improvement steps is finite.

explanations.text: The notes name this property explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: This is called the Finite Improvement Property (FIP).

explanations.text: The corollary in the notes states existence of a pure Nash equilibrium.
explanations.notes_quote: Corollary: Every congestion game has at least one pure Nash equilibrium.

explanations.text: The notes say FIP is stronger than mere existence, because it guarantees convergence of natural dynamics from any start.
explanations.notes_quote: FIP is stronger than just existence of PSNE

explanations.text: This is exactly the stronger interpretation the notes emphasize.
explanations.notes_quote: it means agents can reach equilibrium through natural decentralized dynamics from any starting profile

questions.text: Which statements about Rosenthal's potential function are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It is defined by $\\Phi(a) = \\sum_{j=1}^{m} \\sum_{k=1}^{n_j(a)} d_j(k)$
When one player deviates, the change in $\\Phi$ exactly matches that player's change in cost
Every improvement step strictly increases $\\Phi$
Because $\\Phi$ takes finitely many values over finite profiles, repeated improvements must terminate
Rosenthal's potential is defined only for zero-sum games

explanations.text: The notes give this exact cumulative-delay formula.
The defining proof property is exact equality between potential change and deviator cost change.
explanations.notes_quote: the change in the potential function exactly equals the change in player $i$'s cost

explanations.text: Improvement steps decrease player cost, so they decrease $\\Phi$, not increase it.
explanations.notes_quote: Since each improvement step decreases the player's cost, it also decreases $\\Phi$.

explanations.text: Finite range plus strict decrease yields termination.
explanations.notes_quote: Since $\\Phi$ takes finitely many values (finite strategy profiles), the process must terminate.

explanations.text: Rosenthal's potential is introduced for congestion games, not only zero-sum games.

questions.source_ref: Section 6.2: Exact Potential Games and Section 6.3: Weaker Notions of Potential
questions.text: Which statements correctly distinguish exact, ordinal, and generalized ordinal potential games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In an exact potential game, unilateral cost changes equal potential changes
In an ordinal potential game, only the sign of improvement must match, not the magnitude
In a generalized ordinal potential game, every decrease in cost implies a decrease in potential
The notes state generalized ordinal potential is equivalent to FIP
The notes define ordinal potential by requiring exact equality of all deviations

explanations.text: Exact potential requires equality of unilateral cost change and potential change.
explanations.notes_quote: the change in $\\Phi$ equals the change in player $i$'s cost

explanations.text: Ordinal potential preserves direction of improvement but not magnitude.
explanations.notes_quote: Sign of change matches, but magnitudes need not be equal

explanations.text: Generalized ordinal potential only requires that every improving deviation improves the potential.
explanations.notes_quote: Every improvement in cost implies improvement in $\\Phi$, but not vice versa

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify generalized ordinal potential with FIP.
explanations.notes_quote: Generalized ordinal potential \\equiv Finite Improvement Property (FIP)

explanations.text: Exact equality belongs to exact potential games, not ordinal potential games.

questions.text: Which of the following are correct about identifying potential games in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A potential function is not unique; adding a constant gives another valid one
The guess-and-check method seeds one profile with an arbitrary potential value and derives the rest
For the Prisoner's Dilemma example, the notes derive a consistent potential matrix and conclude it is a potential game
In the cycle approach, every 4-cycle in a potential game must have total deviator utility change summing to 0
If a game has a PSNE, the notes conclude it must be a potential game

explanations.text: The notes explicitly warn that potential functions are never unique up to additive constants.
explanations.notes_quote: The potential function is never unique â€” adding any constant to a potential function gives another valid potential function.

explanations.text: This is the first step of the guess-and-check procedure.
explanations.notes_quote: Seed the potential function at some profile with an arbitrary value

explanations.text: The Prisoner's Dilemma example yields a consistent matrix and is declared a potential game.
explanations.notes_quote: Prisoner's Dilemma is a potential game.

explanations.text: The cycle test requires the sum of deviator utility changes around a 4-cycle to be zero.
explanations.notes_quote: If the game is a potential game, then $\\Delta_1 + \\Delta_2 + \\Delta_3 + \\Delta_4 = 0$ for any cycle of length 4.

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say PSNE existence does not imply the game is a potential game.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Connection: PSNE and Potential Games
explanations.notes_quote: Having a PSNE does not imply the game is a potential game.

questions.source_ref: Section 6.6: Computing PSNE of Congestion Games and Section 7: Maximum Cut Problem and Local Search
questions.text: Which statements correctly connect congestion games, local minima, and local search complexity? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Computing a PSNE of a congestion game is equivalent to finding a local minimum of Rosenthal's potential
The notes use maximum cut as a canonical local-search problem connected to potential-game reasoning
A local optimum of max-cut must also be a global optimum
For unit-weight graphs, max-cut local search terminates in at most $|E|$ iterations
The notes state that finding local maxima of max-cut instances is PLS-complete

explanations.text: This is the exact computational connection drawn in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.6: Computing PSNE of Congestion Games
explanations.notes_quote: Computing a PSNE of a congestion game is equivalent to computing a local minimum of Rosenthal's potential function.

explanations.text: Max-cut is introduced as a canonical local-search problem for this discussion.
explanations.notes_quote: A canonical problem for studying local search and its connection to potential games.

explanations.text: The notes explicitly warn that a local optimum need not be a global optimum.
explanations.notes_quote: A local optimum need not be a global optimum.

explanations.text: For unit weights, the objective can only increase through finitely many integer values up to $|E|$.
explanations.notes_quote: For unit weight graphs: objective takes values in $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, |E|\\}$, so local search terminates in at most $|E|$ iterations

explanations.text: The notes classify local max-cut as PLS-complete.
explanations.notes_quote: Finding local maxima of max-cut instances is PLS-complete

sections.title: 4. Price of Anarchy, Price of Stability, and Tutorial Integration

questions.text: Which statements about Price of Anarchy (PoA) and Price of Stability (PoS) are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For minimization problems, $	ext{PoA} =  rac{	ext{value of worst Nash equilibrium}}{	ext{value of optimal solution}}$
For minimization problems, $	ext{PoS} =  rac{	ext{value of best Nash equilibrium}}{	ext{value of optimal solution}}$
For maximization problems, the notes keep the same fraction orientation without modification
The notes state $	ext{PoS} \\leq 	ext{PoA}$ always
PoA measures the best equilibrium and PoS measures the worst equilibrium

explanations.text: This is the minimization definition given for PoA.
explanations.notes_quote: PoA = $ rac{	ext{value of worst Nash equilibrium}}{	ext{value of optimal solution}}$

explanations.text: This is the minimization definition given for PoS.
explanations.notes_quote: PoS = $ rac{	ext{value of best Nash equilibrium}}{	ext{value of optimal solution}}$

explanations.text: For maximization, the notes say to invert the fractions so the values remain at least 1.
explanations.notes_quote: For maximization, invert the fractions so that PoA, PoS $\\geq 1$.

explanations.text: The best equilibrium cannot be worse than the worst equilibrium, so the notes record $	ext{PoS} \\leq 	ext{PoA}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Always: $	ext{PoS} \\leq 	ext{PoA}$.

explanations.text: These roles are reversed: PoA is worst-case equilibrium efficiency, PoS is best-case equilibrium efficiency.

questions.text: Which of the following example computations from the notes are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma example, PoA = PoS = 10
In Battle of the Sexes, PoS = 1 and PoA = $4/3$
In the routing game example, PoA = PoS = $9/8$
In Battle of the Sexes, the worst equilibrium has welfare 2
In the Prisoner's Dilemma example, the unique equilibrium is socially optimal

explanations.text: The notes compute welfare 20 for the optimum and 2 for the unique equilibrium, giving ratio 10.
explanations.notes_quote: PoA = PoS = 20/2 = 10

explanations.text: The best equilibrium is optimal and the worst equilibrium has welfare 3, so the ratios are 1 and $4/3$.
The routing example compares equilibrium social cost 9 to optimum 8.
The notes say the two equilibria have welfare 3 and 4, not 2.
explanations.notes_quote: Two NE: (Theater,Theater) with welfare 3, (Football,Football) with welfare 4

explanations.text: The Prisoner's Dilemma example is used precisely because the unique equilibrium is inefficient relative to the optimum.
explanations.notes_quote: Unique NE: (D,D) with social welfare = 2; Optimal: (C,C) with social welfare = 20

questions.source_ref: Section 8.3: Simple Two-Link Example and Section 8.4: Price of Anarchy for Linear Congestion Games
questions.text: Which statements about the two-link example and the linear-congestion PoA theorem are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the two-link example, the equilibrium puts all $n$ players on the upper link with social cost $n^2$
Splitting players evenly gives social cost $ rac{3n^2}{4}$ in the notes
For linear congestion games, the notes state PoA is at most $5/2$ and this bound is tight
The tight example in the notes has optimal total cost 10 and worst equilibrium total cost 4
The two-link example shows that the equilibrium can be worse than a more balanced allocation

explanations.text: The identity-cost upper link attracts everyone at equilibrium, yielding total cost $n \\cdot n = n^2$.
explanations.notes_quote: Equilibrium: All $n$ players take upper link â†’ social cost = $n \\cdot n = n^2$

explanations.text: The notes compute the balanced split cost as $ rac{3n^2}{4}$.
explanations.notes_quote: social cost = $ rac{n}{2} \\cdot  rac{n}{2} +  rac{n}{2} \\cdot n =  rac{3n^2}{4}$

explanations.text: The theorem is stated exactly with tight bound $5/2$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: Price of Anarchy for Linear Congestion Games
explanations.notes_quote: the Price of Anarchy is at most 5/2 ... This bound is tight

explanations.text: The notes give optimal cost 4 and worst equilibrium cost 10, not the reverse.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: Price of Anarchy for Linear Congestion Games
explanations.notes_quote: Optimal solution ... total cost = 4; Worst NE ... total cost = 10

explanations.text: That is the point of the example: selfish routing can overload the identity-cost link.
explanations.notes_quote: Better split: $n/2$ on each

questions.source_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function and Section 8.6: Price of Stability with Bounded Congestion
questions.text: Which of the following are consequences of the potential-based bounds in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For linear congestion games, the notes prove $ rac{1}{2}\\mathrm{SC}(s) \\leq \\Phi(s) \\leq \\mathrm{SC}(s)$
The upper bound uses nondecreasing cost functions to compare cumulative costs with full congestion costs
If no resource is used by more than $\\lambda$ players, then PoS is at most $\\lambda$
The bounded-congestion proof starts from an arbitrary equilibrium and runs best response dynamics toward the optimum
The notes use the chain $\\mathrm{SC}(s) \\leq \\lambda \\Phi(s) \\leq \\lambda \\Phi(s^*) \\leq \\lambda \\mathrm{SC}(s^*)$

explanations.text: This is the theorem stated for linear congestion games.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
explanations.notes_quote: For linear congestion games, $ rac{1}{2}	ext{SC}(s) \\leq \\Phi(s) \\leq 	ext{SC}(s)$

explanations.text: The upper-bound proof uses monotonicity of the cost functions to replace cumulative terms by the maximum congestion term.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
explanations.notes_quote: Since cost functions are nondecreasing: $\\Phi(s) ... \\leq ... = 	ext{SC}(s)$

explanations.text: This is the bounded-congestion PoS theorem.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: Price of Stability with Bounded Congestion
explanations.notes_quote: the Price of Stability is at most $\\lambda$

explanations.text: The proof starts from the optimal profile $s^*$ and runs best response dynamics to an equilibrium, not the other way around.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: Price of Stability with Bounded Congestion
explanations.notes_quote: Start from optimal profile $s^*$. If it's a NE, PoS = 1. Otherwise, apply best response dynamics to reach NE $s$.

explanations.text: The notes present exactly this inequality chain in the proof.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: Price of Stability with Bounded Congestion
explanations.notes_quote: $$	ext{SC}(s) \\leq \\lambda \\Phi(s) \\leq \\lambda \\Phi(s^*) \\leq \\lambda \\cdot 	ext{SC}(s^*)$$

questions.source_ref: Section 9.1: Exercise 1: Network Congestion Game PSNE
questions.text: In the tutorial PSNE exercise, which of the following statements are true? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The initial state has all three players on route $s 	o v_3 	o t$
One improvement step has Player 3 switch to $s 	o v_1 	o t$
The final PSNE sends all three players through $s 	o v_2 	o v_1 	o t$
All PSNE in the game send two players via $s 	o v_1 	o t$ and one via $s 	o v_2 	o v_1 	o t$
The exercise illustrates finding PSNE by best response dynamics

explanations.text: The solution starts from the profile where all players use the same route through $v_3$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.1: Exercise 1: Network Congestion Game PSNE
explanations.notes_quote: Start with all players on route $s 	o v_3 	o t$

explanations.text: This is one of the listed improvement steps in the sequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.1: Exercise 1: Network Congestion Game PSNE
explanations.notes_quote: (c) Player 3 switches to $s 	o v_1 	o t$

explanations.text: The final PSNE has one player on $s 	o v_2 	o v_1 	o t$ and two on $s 	o v_1 	o t$, not all three on the longer route.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.1: Exercise 1: Network Congestion Game PSNE
explanations.notes_quote: All PSNE in this game send two players via $s 	o v_1 	o t$ and one via $s 	o v_2 	o v_1 	o t$.

explanations.text: The notes summarize the full set of PSNE in exactly this way.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.1: Exercise 1: Network Congestion Game PSNE
explanations.notes_quote: All PSNE in this game send two players via $s 	o v_1 	o t$ and one via $s 	o v_2 	o v_1 	o t$.

explanations.text: The exercise is explicitly framed as finding PSNE by iterative best responses.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.1: Exercise 1: Network Congestion Game PSNE
explanations.notes_quote: Find PSNE by best response dynamics.

questions.text: Which of the following integrative conclusions are supported by the Week 2 notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Congestion games combine concise modeling with guaranteed convergence of improvement dynamics to a pure equilibrium
Potential functions help both prove equilibrium existence and connect congestion games to local-search complexity
Equilibrium existence alone guarantees socially optimal outcomes in congestion games
PoA and PoS quantify how far selfish equilibria can be from optimal social outcomes
Braess's Paradox shows that adding resources can worsen equilibrium performance
Every game with a PSNE must be an exact potential game

explanations.text: This combines the concise-representation motivation with Rosenthal's finite-improvement result.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Motivation: Large Games and Concise Representations
explanations.notes_quote: games with a concise description that makes them easy to reason about

explanations.text: The notes use potential functions both for convergence proofs and to motivate local-search complexity questions.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.6: Computing PSNE of Congestion Games
explanations.notes_quote: Computing a PSNE of a congestion game is equivalent to computing a local minimum of Rosenthal's potential function.

explanations.text: The routing example and Braess's paradox both show equilibria can be inefficient relative to the social optimum.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.4: Worked Example: Three-Player Routing Game
explanations.notes_quote: State B is the Nash equilibrium despite having higher social cost. State A is the social optimum.

explanations.text: That is exactly the role of PoA and PoS in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Given an optimization goal ... we define efficiency measures

explanations.text: Braess's paradox is the canonical example of extra resources worsening equilibrium outcomes.
explanations.notes_quote: Adding a new road (resource) to a network can increase the total travel time at equilibrium.

explanations.text: The notes explicitly reject this implication: PSNE existence does not imply being a potential game.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Connection: PSNE and Potential Games
explanations.notes_quote: Having a PSNE does not imply the game is a potential game.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.6: IoT and Sensor Networks and Section 3.7: Other Applications
questions.text: Which additional application areas are explicitly listed in the expanded Week 2 notes beyond the first five application domains? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: IoT and sensor networks
Crowdsourcing and shared economy platforms
Resource allocation in AI/ML
Cybersecurity
Urban planning and facility location
Only repeated auctions with no shared resources

explanations.text: IoT and sensor networks are explicitly added as a separate application subsection.
explanations.notes_quote: Devices compete for transmission time, battery usage, or shared sensors.

explanations.text: The notes list crowdsourcing and shared-economy platforms as congestion-style resource-allocation settings.
explanations.notes_quote: Crowdsourcing and Shared Economy: Platforms allocate resources across a city; too many users at one location reduces utility for all

explanations.text: AI/ML resource allocation is explicitly named in the expanded notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Resource Allocation in AI/ML: Competing AI agents select computing resources

explanations.text: Cybersecurity is included as a domain where attackers and defenders allocate resources.
explanations.notes_quote: Cybersecurity: Attackers and defenders allocate resources across network surfaces

explanations.text: Urban planning and facility location are listed because overuse of public resources degrades quality.
explanations.notes_quote: Urban Planning and Facility Location: Citizens choose between public resources; overuse degrades quality

explanations.text: The notes emphasize shared-resource congestion settings, not only repeated auctions without congestion effects.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.1: The Algorithm and Section 5.2: Rosenthal's Theorem (1973)
questions.text: Which statements correctly distinguish best responses, improvement steps, and the finite improvement property? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A best response is an action minimizing a player's cost against the current actions of others
An improvement step is any unilateral deviation that strictly lowers the deviating player's cost
FIP means every sequence of unilateral deviations, including non-improving ones, is finite
Rosenthal's theorem is about sequences of improvement steps, not arbitrary deviations
If a game has FIP, repeated improving deviations cannot cycle forever

explanations.text: This is exactly how the algorithm defines the update target.
explanations.notes_quote: argmin_{a'\in A_i} c_i(a', a_{-i})

explanations.text: Improvement steps are profitable unilateral deviations that reduce cost.
explanations.notes_quote: every sequence of improvement steps is finite

explanations.text: FIP concerns improving deviations only; arbitrary deviations need not satisfy the property.
explanations.notes_quote: every sequence of improvement steps is finite

explanations.text: The theorem is stated specifically for improvement paths.
explanations.notes_quote: For every congestion game, every sequence of improvement steps is finite.

explanations.text: That is the operational meaning of FIP: no infinite improvement cycle exists.
explanations.notes_quote: This is called the Finite Improvement Property (FIP).

questions.text: Which statements about the guess-and-check construction for potential games are correct according to the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: One may seed the potential at an arbitrary profile with an arbitrary value
Consistency of derived values across different derivation paths is essential
If inconsistency arises, the game cannot be an exact potential game under that deviation structure
The notes use Prisoner's Dilemma to show a game with no valid potential function
Adding a constant to every potential value preserves validity

explanations.text: The notes explicitly start by assigning an arbitrary seed value.
explanations.notes_quote: Seed the potential function at some profile with an arbitrary value

explanations.text: The whole method depends on deriving the same value regardless of the path used.
explanations.notes_quote: If consistent values are obtained, the game is a potential game

explanations.text: An inconsistency is exactly the certificate that the potential equations cannot all hold simultaneously.
explanations.notes_quote: If inconsistency arises, it is not a potential game

explanations.text: Prisoner's Dilemma is used as a positive example that does admit a potential function.
explanations.notes_quote: Prisoner's Dilemma is a potential game.

explanations.text: Potential functions are only defined up to an additive constant.
explanations.notes_quote: adding any constant to a potential function gives another valid potential function

questions.text: Which statements about the cycle-based test for potential games are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The test examines 4-cycles formed by alternating unilateral deviations of two players
For a potential game, the sum of deviator utility changes around such a cycle must be 0
The notes give an example with cycle sum 4, concluding the game is not a potential game
A nonzero cycle sum is compatible with exact potential games as long as the game has a PSNE
For 2-player 2-strategy games, checking all 4-cycles proves the result

explanations.text: The notes write the cycle explicitly as alternating unilateral deviations by the two players.
explanations.notes_quote: Consider cycles of action profiles of the form: $$(a_i, a_j, \cdot) \to (a'_i, a_j, \cdot) \to (a'_i, a'_j, \cdot) \to (a_i, a'_j, \cdot) \to (a_i, a_j, \cdot)$$

explanations.text: This zero-sum-around-the-cycle condition is the core test.
explanations.notes_quote: If the game is a potential game, then $\Delta_1 + \Delta_2 + \Delta_3 + \Delta_4 = 0$

explanations.text: The worked example has cycle sum 4 and is used as a negative example.
A nonzero cycle sum violates the potential condition regardless of PSNE existence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.5: Connection: PSNE and Potential Games
explanations.notes_quote: A game can have PSNE but fail the cycle test

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state this suffices in the 2x2 case.
explanations.notes_quote: Checking all cycles of length 4 proves the result (for 2-player games with 2 strategies each).

questions.source_ref: Section 7.1: The Max-Cut Problem and Section 7.2: Local Search Algorithm
questions.text: Which statements about the maximum cut problem and its local search formulation are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A feasible solution is a partition $(S, \bar{S})$ of the vertices into two non-empty sets
The objective is to maximize the total weight of edges crossing the cut
A local move consists of moving one vertex to the other side while keeping both sides non-empty
The notes state maximum cut is polynomial-time solvable because local search always finds the global optimum
The change in objective when moving a vertex compares newly cut weight against newly uncut weight

explanations.text: This is the exact feasible-solution definition in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Cuts $(S, \bar{S})$, where $(S, \bar{S})$ is a partition of $V$ into two non-empty sets

explanations.text: The objective is the total weight of cut edges.
explanations.notes_quote: Maximize the total weight of cut edges

explanations.text: The local neighborhood is defined by single-vertex moves.
explanations.notes_quote: A local move = moving a vertex $v$ from one side of the cut to the other

explanations.text: The notes say maximum cut is NP-hard and local optima need not be global optima.
explanations.notes_quote: Unlike minimum cut, maximum cut is NP-hard.

explanations.text: The formula explicitly subtracts newly uncut weight from newly cut weight.
explanations.notes_quote: $$\sum_{u \in S} w_{uv} - \sum_{u \in \bar{S}} w_{uv}$$

questions.source_ref: Section 7.3: Convergence Properties and Section 7.4: PLS: Abstract Local Search Problems
questions.text: Which statements about local-search complexity are supported by the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For unit-weight graphs, max-cut local search terminates in at most |E| iterations
For general weights, the notes state there is no known polynomial-time algorithm for computing local optima
PLS-complete is presented as the local-search analogue of NP-complete
An abstract local search problem is specified by exactly three polynomial-time algorithms
The notes rule out exponential lower bounds for local search

explanations.text: This follows from the bounded integer range of the objective in unit-weight graphs.
explanations.notes_quote: local search terminates in at most $|E|$ iterations

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state the absence of a known polynomial-time algorithm in the general-weight case.
explanations.notes_quote: For general weights: no known polynomial-time algorithm for computing local optima

explanations.text: This analogy is stated directly in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.4: PLS: Abstract Local Search Problems
explanations.notes_quote: PLS-completeness is the local search analogue of NP-completeness

explanations.text: The notes enumerate three poly-time procedures: initial solution, objective evaluation, and local-improvement check.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.4: PLS: Abstract Local Search Problems
explanations.notes_quote: An abstract local search problem is specified by 3 poly-time algorithms

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: exponential lower bounds do exist.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.4: PLS: Abstract Local Search Problems
explanations.notes_quote: Exponential lower bounds exist on the number of iterations required by local search

questions.source_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
questions.text: Which statements about the proof ingredients for bounding potential versus social cost in linear congestion games are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The upper bound uses the fact that resource cost functions are nondecreasing
The lower bound uses the inequality $\frac{n^{k+1}}{k+1} \leq \sum_{j=1}^{n} j^k$ for $k=0,1$
The lower bound also uses linearity of the form $c_r(x)=a_r x + b_r$
The theorem concludes $\Phi(s) \leq \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{SC}(s)$ for all linear congestion games
The notes state both $\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{SC}(s) \leq \Phi(s)$ and $\Phi(s) \leq \mathrm{SC}(s)$

explanations.text: Monotonicity is the key step in the upper-bound argument.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
explanations.notes_quote: Since cost functions are nondecreasing

explanations.text: The notes cite this inequality explicitly for the lower bound.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
explanations.notes_quote: Uses the inequality $\frac{n^{k+1}}{k+1} \leq \sum_{j=1}^{n} j^k$ for $k = 0, 1$

explanations.text: Linearity of the resource costs is another explicit proof ingredient.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
explanations.notes_quote: and the linearity of cost functions $c_r(x) = a_r \cdot x + b_r$

explanations.text: The inequality goes the other way: $\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{SC}(s) \leq \Phi(s)$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
explanations.notes_quote: For linear congestion games, $\frac{1}{2}\text{SC}(s) \leq \Phi(s) \leq \text{SC}(s)$

explanations.text: This is the theorem statement itself.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Bounding PoS via Potential Function (for Linear Congestion Games)
explanations.notes_quote: $\frac{1}{2}\text{SC}(s) \leq \Phi(s) \leq \text{SC}(s)$

questions.source_ref: Section 9.2: Exercise 2: Potential vs Social Cost in Linear Games and Section 9.3: Exercise 3: PoS with Bounded Congestion
questions.text: Which tutorial exercise conclusions are explicitly stated in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Exercise 2 concludes $\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{SC}(s) \leq \Phi(s) \leq \mathrm{SC}(s)$ for linear congestion games
Exercise 3 concludes that if no resource is used by more than $\lambda$ players, then PoS \leq \lambda
Exercise 2 concludes PoA = 2 for all congestion games
Exercise 3 is about proving every congestion game is zero-sum
These exercises reinforce the theorem statements from Section 8 rather than introducing unrelated material

explanations.text: Exercise 2 restates the potential-versus-social-cost bound.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.2: Exercise 2: Potential vs Social Cost in Linear Games
explanations.notes_quote: $\frac{1}{2}\text{SC}(s) \leq \Phi(s) \leq \text{SC}(s)$ for linear congestion games

explanations.text: Exercise 3 restates the bounded-congestion PoS theorem.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.3: Exercise 3: PoS with Bounded Congestion
explanations.notes_quote: If no resource is used by more than $\lambda$ players, PoS \leq \lambda

explanations.text: No such universal PoA = 2 statement appears for all congestion games.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.2: Exercise 2: Potential vs Social Cost in Linear Games

explanations.text: Exercise 3 concerns Price of Stability under bounded congestion, not zero-sum structure.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.3: Exercise 3: PoS with Bounded Congestion

explanations.text: Both exercises are direct tutorial reinforcements of the preceding theorem material.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: Price of Stability with Bounded Congestion
explanations.notes_quote: *Addresses LO7, LO8*

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe cut games as introduced in the tutorial section? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Each player controls a vertex of an undirected graph
Each player's strategy is LEFT or RIGHT
Player $i$'s utility is the total weight of cut edges adjacent to their vertex
The game is defined on directed graphs only
The cut is the partition induced by the players' LEFT/RIGHT choices

explanations.text: The notes assign one player to each vertex.
explanations.notes_quote: Undirected graph $G = (V,E)$, each player $i$ controls vertex $v_i$

explanations.text: The strategy set is exactly the two sides of the cut.
explanations.notes_quote: Strategy: LEFT or RIGHT

explanations.text: Utility is defined by the weight of adjacent edges that cross the cut.
explanations.notes_quote: Player $i$'s utility = total weight of cut edges adjacent to $v_i$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly define cut games on undirected graphs.
explanations.notes_quote: Undirected graph $G = (V,E)$

explanations.text: LEFT/RIGHT choices induce the partition and therefore the cut.
explanations.notes_quote: Cut = partition into two sets

questions.text: Which statements about the cut-game Price of Anarchy example and proof are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The notes give a 4-vertex example with PoA = 2
In that example, the equilibrium cut has total payoff 4 and the optimal cut has total payoff 8
The notes prove PoA can exceed 2 by constructing a 6-vertex counterexample
The proof of PoA \leq 2 uses the fact that in any PSNE each player's cut-edge weight is at least half the total weight of incident edges
The optimal total cut value cannot exceed twice the sum of edge weights counted once

explanations.text: The tutorial explicitly labels part (a) as a PoA = 2 example.
explanations.notes_quote: (a) PoA = 2 example

explanations.text: Those are the exact values used to compute the ratio 8/4 = 2.
explanations.notes_quote: NE ... payoff 4. Optimal ... payoff 8. PoA = 8/4 = 2.

explanations.text: The notes prove the opposite upper bound, namely PoA \leq 2.
explanations.notes_quote: (b) PoA \leq 2 proof

explanations.text: This half-of-incident-weight argument is the key equilibrium inequality in the proof.
explanations.notes_quote: In any PSNE, each player's cut edges are at least half the total weight of their adjacent edges

explanations.text: The proof bounds the optimum by counting each edge at most twice across players.
explanations.notes_quote: The optimal can't exceed $2 \sum_{e \in E} w_e$ (each edge counted twice)

questions.text: Which of the following broader exam-level takeaways are explicitly supported by the full Week 2 notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Congestion-style reasoning applies well beyond traffic, including AI/ML, IoT, and cybersecurity
Potential functions are useful not only for equilibrium existence proofs but also for analyzing local-search complexity
Local optima and Nash equilibria are always globally optimal in the settings studied this week
Efficiency analysis requires comparing equilibrium outcomes with social optima via PoA and PoS
Tutorial cut games extend the week's efficiency-analysis theme beyond standard routing examples
Braess's paradox shows that more resources always improve decentralized outcomes

explanations.text: The expanded applications section explicitly broadens the scope beyond transportation.
explanations.notes_quote: Resource Allocation in AI/ML ... Cybersecurity ... Urban Planning and Facility Location

explanations.text: This connection is made directly when PSNE computation is linked to local minima of the potential.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.6: Computing PSNE of Congestion Games
explanations.notes_quote: complexity theory for local search problems is immediately relevant

explanations.text: The notes repeatedly stress that equilibria and local optima can be inefficient relative to global optima.
explanations.notes_quote: A local optimum need not be a global optimum.

explanations.text: That is the explicit purpose of PoA and PoS.
explanations.notes_quote: we define efficiency measures

explanations.text: The cut-game exercise applies the same equilibrium-versus-optimum lens in a different game family.
Braess's paradox says the opposite can happen: extra resources may worsen equilibrium outcomes.
explanations.notes_quote: Adding a new road (resource) to a network can increase the total travel time at equilibrium.
